articles.title: These Ukrainians Don’t Want to Be Traded to Russia for Peace
articles.summary: Four years into the war, a major sticking point in talks is control of the eastern Donetsk region. Residents could face an agonizing choice if Ukraine gives up the territory.

articles.title: Mexico Is Caught Between Trump and the Cartels
articles.summary: President Trump has demanded President Claudia Sheinbaum confront the cartels. The killing of El Mencho suggests it might be working — but could come at a cost.

articles.title: Iranians Brace for War as Trump Considers Targeted Strikes
articles.summary: Stores are well stocked, and there have been no reports of shortages of food and other essentials, but many Iranians are in limbo as they wait to see whether U.S. forces will attack.

articles.title: Louvre Director Resigns, Months After Crown Jewels Were Stolen in Heist
articles.summary: Laurence des Cars’s departure is the latest setback for the world’s largest museum. Her tenure was marred by labor strikes, water leaks, a ticket scam and security lapses, which led to the heist in October.

articles.title: Study Shows Xi’s Purges of China’s Military Run Deep
articles.summary: Around 100 senior officers have been sidelined or vanished since 2022, hollowing out the top ranks and raising questions about the army’s capabilities.

articles.title: What to Know About the Cartels Operating in Mexico
articles.summary: Other criminal groups in Mexico may try to take advantage of the death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, also known as El Mencho, who ran the Jalisco New Generation Cartel.

articles.title: Germany’s Leader Heads to China and Walks a Tightrope Between Xi and Trump
articles.summary: Chancellor Friedrich Merz’s trip will test his ability to address tensions between the countries, at a time of strain between Europe and Washington.

articles.title: Ukraine Battlefield Dead Could Reach 500,000 in Fifth Year, Estimates Suggest
articles.summary: Russia has lost as many as 325,000 troops, according to some estimates, with more than 200,000 deaths verified by researchers.

articles.title: Russia Remade Its Economy for War, but It’s Come at a Huge Cost
articles.summary: About half of the country’s federal budget goes toward the fight in Ukraine, money that does little to support its long-term development.

articles.title: Russia Moves Closer to Banning Telegram App, Saying It Foments Terrorism
articles.summary: News of an investigation into Pavel Durov, the app’s billionaire founder, deepened a crackdown on what remains of the free Russian internet.

articles.title: Mandelson’s Arrest Adds to Scandal That Has Rattled Starmer and Labour Party
articles.summary: The arrest of the British former envoy to Washington, long a key figure in the Labour Party, deepened a scandal that has led to calls for Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s resignation.

articles.title: How Bookbinders Helped the Nazis Track Holocaust Victims
articles.summary: Conservation experts helped the Nazi regime inspect church and civil archives to track down people they sought to persecute, a researcher concluded.

articles.title: Norway’s Century-Long Watch on the Northern Lights
articles.summary: The nation’s northern region has led the scientific quest to understand the aurora borealis. This summer, a 10,000-antenna radar is expected to begin the next phase of exploration.

articles.title: What Brontë Country Tells Us About Britain Today
articles.summary: West Yorkshire, where the original “Wuthering Heights” was written, offers a window into the forces that are disrupting British politics.

articles.title: In Windsor, Some See Family Drama in the Andrew Accusations
articles.summary: “Her children have let her down,” said one woman, referring to Queen Elizabeth II. Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor’s fall from grace drew strong reactions from some. Others said they just did not care.

articles.title: Futsal Phenom in Afghanistan Is a Hero the Taliban Didn’t Expect
articles.summary: A triumph in indoor soccer has turned Alireza Ahmadi, 17, and other players from the Hazara minority, long marginalized in Afghanistan, into national heroes.

articles.title: Same War, Different Message: Trump Tonight vs. Biden Four Years Ago
articles.summary: President Trump issued no statement in support of Ukraine on the anniversary of the Russian invasion, four years after his predecessor laid out the stakes.

articles.title: Trump Squeezes Cuba
articles.summary: Many U.S. presidents have tried to bring down the Communist government over almost seven decades. This time, it feels different.

articles.title: How the U.S. Is Crippling Cuba’s Economy
articles.summary: Cuba is facing its worst economic crisis in 67 years. Blackouts and fuel shortages have worsened after President Trump tightened restrictions on oil. Our international correspondent Frances Robles talks with Katrin Bennhold about the current situation in Cuba.

articles.title: Airlines Add Flights After Storm and Snow in Northeast Cause Cancellations
articles.summary: The major U.S. airlines said they would add more flights in airports that serve cities like Boston, New York and Philadelphia.

articles.title: China Amps Up Pressure on Japan With Restrictions on Exports
articles.summary: Beijing placed the restrictions on 20 Japanese entities with ties to the defense industry, the latest ratcheting up of its monthslong feud with Tokyo.

articles.title: Is It Safe to Travel to Mexico Right Now, Given the Cartel Violence?
articles.summary: A wave of unrest after the killing of a cartel leader has rattled tourists in Mexico, prompting travelers to reconsider their plans. Here’s what to know.

articles.title: After Six Decades of War on Drugs, Does Anything Work?
articles.summary: The U.S. and its allies have spilled blood and treasure to kill drug lords and defeat cartels, but the drugs keep coming and the new groups are more violent than ever.

articles.title: The Looming Taiwan Chip Disaster That Silicon Valley Has Long Ignored
articles.summary: If China invades Taiwan and cuts off its chip exports to American companies, the tech industry and the U.S. economy would be crippled.

articles.title: ‘Mother Russia’ Review: A New Play That’s as Funny as It’s Smart
articles.summary: A strong cast stars in Lauren Yee’s new play, part of a cycle of works about the collisions between 20th-century communism, capitalism and pop culture.

articles.title: After Chaos Rips Across Mexico, the Remnants of Cartel Violence Haunt Residents
articles.summary: Charred vehicles cluttered deserted streets as residents started to emerge from their homes in Guadalajara, which was hit hard by violence after the death of a cartel leader.

articles.title: Hunkering Down in Mexico, Some Hope for a Flight Out Amid Burning Cars
articles.summary: People from Canada and the United States tell of scrambling for safety, food and flights after violence erupted in Mexico when a cartel boss died in custody.

articles.title: Canada to Probe What OpenAI Knew About Tumbler Ridge Shooter
articles.summary: The company suspended the killer’s ChatGPT account over a policy violation in June, eight months before the attacks in Tumbler Ridge, British Columbia.

articles.title: Mexican Forces Say They Tracked El Mencho to Cabin by Following His Lover
articles.summary: Top security officials revealed details of the operation that led to the death of Mexico’s most wanted drug cartel leader.

articles.title: C.I.A. Intelligence Helped Lead Mexican Authorities to ‘El Mencho’
articles.summary: Mexican officials said they had found the elusive cartel kingpin by tracking a romantic partner. The C.I.A. provided some intelligence critical to the operation.

articles.title: Europe’s New Way of War
articles.summary: Can Europe defend itself without the United States? A new strategic vision says yes.

articles.title: Videos Show How Violence Unfolded in Mexico After Killing of Cartel Boss
articles.summary: Armed groups blocked roads and set fire to supermarkets and banks after the Mexican government killed Mexico’s most-wanted cartel boss.

articles.title: Who Is Peter Mandelson, the UK Ex-Ambassador Fired Over His Epstein Ties?
articles.summary: The former British ambassador to the United States was arrested on Monday, months after being fired over his ties to Jeffrey Epstein, the convicted sex offender.

articles.title: Settlers in the Israeli-Occupied West Bank Drive a Palestinian Family Off Its Land
articles.summary: For two years, Jewish settlers attacked Rezeq Abu Naim’s land in the Israeli-occupied West Bank at all hours and in all manners. After another violent incursion over the weekend, his family abandoned their home.

articles.title: Peter Mandelson, Ex-Ambassador to the U.S., Is Released After U.K. Arrest Amid Epstein Accusations
articles.summary: Peter Mandelson was arrested on Monday on suspicion of “misconduct in public office” following revelations about his dealings with Jeffrey Epstein, the sex offender.

articles.title: The European Union Hits Pause on Its U.S. Trade Deal
articles.summary: The E.U. isn’t throwing out the deal it agreed with the United States before President Trump’s tariffs were overruled, but European officials want more clarity first.

articles.title: Trump Administration Scrambles to Pick Up the Pieces of Broken Tariffs
articles.summary: President Trump is already working to piece his tariff program back together, after a Supreme Court ruling ruptured a centerpiece of his economic agenda.

articles.title: Puerto Vallarta Jolted By Violence After Cartel Boss Killing
articles.summary: When violence broke out after the killing of Mexico’s most-wanted cartel boss, vacation spots ordinarily spared from the drug wars were also targeted.

articles.title: France Summons U.S. Ambassador Over Comments on Activist’s Killing
articles.summary: Charles Kushner, President Trump’s envoy to Paris, was called in after the State Department cited “violent radical leftism” in the beating death of Quentin Deranque, 23.

articles.title: Supreme Court to Weigh Oil-Industry Effort to End a Major Climate Suit
articles.summary: The case could have significant bearing on a range of other lawsuits brought against the fossil fuel industry by cities and states across the country.

articles.title: Here’s the latest.
EU Support for Ukraine Stumbles as Hungary Looks to Delay Aid
articles.summary: Hungary said that it would block both the latest sanctions package on Russia and a financial aid package to Kyiv worth about $106 billion.

articles.title: Women’s Olympic Hockey Gold Medalist Has Her Brothers’ Eccentric Support
articles.summary: The three brothers of Haley Winn, a defender for the U.S. squad, gained a spotlight for the colorful ways they cheered on their sister’s dream.

articles.title: For Iran’s Rulers, Refusing U.S. Demands Is a Risk Worth Taking
articles.summary: The government in Tehran sees capitulating to Washington’s demands on uranium enrichment and ballistic missiles as riskier to its survival than going to war, analysts say.

articles.title: Supreme Court Considers Fate of Docks and Other Assets Seized by Cuba in 1960
articles.summary: Amid rising tensions with Cuba, the Trump administration is backing lawsuits that would allow Americans to get compensation for property confiscated by Fidel Castro’s regime.

articles.title: The Ukrainian Bureaucrat Working to Squeeze Russia’s War Machine
articles.summary: Vladyslav Vlasiuk has spent the past four years pressing Western allies to squeeze the Russian economy through more sanctions. He hopes that 2026 will be the tipping point.

articles.title: The Japanese Airport That Doesn’t Lose Bags
articles.summary: Kansai International Airport, which is located near Osaka, Japan, hasn’t lost a single piece of luggage since it opened in 1994. River Akira Davis, our Tokyo correspondent, visited the airport to understand how Japanese culture has influenced its success.

articles.title: The Zero Units Fought for the C.I.A. in Afghanistan. In the U.S., They’re Living in Fear.
articles.summary: A shooting in Washington, D.C., threw their immigration status into jeopardy — and brought attention to a long-hidden dimension of America’s war.

articles.title: Drug Lord ‘El Mencho’ Was Killed in Mexico. What’s Next for the Cartel He Led?
articles.summary: The death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes dealt a major blow to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, but few believe it spells the end for the powerful group.

articles.title: Duterte Won’t Appear at Hearing as I.C.C. Weighs Evidence Against Him
articles.summary: The former leader of the Philippines faces a possible trial for the deaths of thousands during his war on drugs. Supporters and opponents gathered in the Netherlands as a key hearing started.

articles.title: Tour Montparnasse Is One of Paris’s Uglier Landmarks. It’s Getting a Makeover.
articles.summary: The Tour Montparnasse is one of Paris’s least-loved landmarks. After half a century, it’s finally being remodeled.

articles.title: What to Know About the Killing of ‘El Mencho’
articles.summary: The Mexican government has killed Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, the longtime leader of one of Mexico’s most powerful gangs, the Jalisco New Generation Cartel.

articles.title: Trump Considers Targeted Strike Against Iran, Followed by Larger Attack
articles.summary: The top negotiators plan to meet in Geneva on Thursday for last-ditch talks, debating a new proposal that could create an off-ramp as two carrier groups massed within striking distance of Iran.

articles.title: Courting China Again
articles.summary: The German chancellor will travel to Beijing this week, the latest high-level visitor as Western countries pivot back to China.

articles.title: ‘This Has Been a Day:’ U.S.-Canada Hockey Game Puts Rivalry on the Line
articles.summary: Canadians and Americans jammed bars that opened early on Sunday to watch the United States deal the Canadian team, and the country, a devastating blow in overtime.
